context.projectOverview: Task inbox FINAL sprint 6: `bober task from-gmail <thread>` egress-gated bonus. A gmail egress axis defaults FALSE (EgressGuard opt-in pattern); axis off => refuse with opt-in message + exitCode=1 + ZERO network/MCP construction. Axis on => read one thread via ExternalMcpServer.callTool, parse LOCALLY (pure parseGmailThread: subject->title, kind=action, status=open), capture via sprint-1 captureTask. Connector errors caught + sanitized (strip KEY=VALUE) + exitCode=1, never leak env/tokens.

completedSprints.title: Capture
completedSprints.summary: src/hub/task-inbox.ts captureTask(store, text, {domain?, now}) is the SINGLE write path reused here.

completedSprints.title: List/lifecycle/snooze/ingest/chat-capture
completedSprints.summary: task list/start/done/drop/snooze/ingest + chat capture-task. All additive.

contract.title: Gmail thread to task (egress-gated bonus)
contract.description: Add `bober task from-gmail <thread>` which, only when an opt-in gmail egress axis is enabled, reads a Gmail thread through the MCP connector, parses it locally into an action-Finding (title from the thread subject), and captures it via captureTask. With the axis off (default) the command refuses with a clear opt-in message and makes no network call.

successCriteria.description: `npm run build` exits 0 with the from-gmail subcommand and the opt-in gmail egress gate added.
A unit test runs from-gmail with the gmail axis disabled (default) and asserts the command returns an opt-in refusal, sets exitCode=1, and never constructs/starts an MCP client (no network).
A unit test runs from-gmail with the axis enabled and a stubbed MCP client returning a thread payload, asserting one open action-Finding is captured with title derived from the thread subject.
A unit test simulates an MCP/connector error and asserts the handler catches it, sets exitCode=1, and the error message contains no connector env/token contents.
`npm run typecheck` exits 0 — the Gmail-thread parser returns a typed Finding accepted by captureTask.

nonGoals: Do NOT enable Gmail egress by default — the axis must default false and gate every network call.
Do NOT build a general Gmail integration/sync — read one thread on demand and parse it locally.
Do NOT include connector env vars or tokens in any error/log message (mirror src/mcp/external-client.ts:66-77).
Do NOT bypass captureTask — the parsed thread is captured through the sprint-1 write path.
Do NOT add new runtime dependencies beyond the already-present MCP SDK.
stopConditions: `npm run build` and `npm run typecheck` exit 0 and the gated/enabled/error from-gmail unit tests pass.
from-gmail refuses with no network when the axis is off and captures a task when the axis is on with a stubbed connector.
contract.definitionOfDone: A user who has explicitly enabled the gmail egress axis can run `bober task from-gmail <thread>` to pull a Gmail thread through the MCP connector, have it parsed locally into an open action-Finding (title from the subject), and captured into the hub pool. With the axis off (the default), the command refuses with an opt-in message and performs zero Gmail egress. Connector failures are caught, reported on stderr with exitCode=1, and never leak env/token contents.
assumptions: A gmail egress axis follows the EgressGuard opt-in pattern (default false, assertAllowed throws if not enabled) — src/medical/egress.ts:1-59.

The Gmail MCP connector is invoked via the ExternalMcpServer.callTool surface (src/mcp/external-client.ts:95-103); the tool name and arg shape are read from listTools at runtime, and the connector is injectable so tests can stub it.
Thread parsing is a pure local function: payload -> {title:<subject>, kind:'action', status:'open'}; it does not call the network itself.
outOfScope: Telegram document upload / mobile capture (telegram scope).
Calendar event creation from threads (calendar scope).
Ongoing Gmail polling or label automation.
contract.generatorNotes: Create src/hub/gmail-to-task.ts with: (1) a pure parseGmailThread(payload) -> Finding-shaped object (title from subject, kind='action', status='open', tags maybe ['source:gmail']); (2) fromGmailTask({egressAllowed, mcp, threadRef, store, now}) that returns/throws an opt-in error when egressAllowed is false BEFORE touching mcp, else mcp.callTool(<gmail-read-tool>, {thread:threadRef}), parse, and captureTask. Add a `from-gmail <thread>` subcommand to src/cli/commands/task.ts that resolves the gmail axis from config (default false), and on disabled prints a chalk.yellow opt-in message + exitCode=1 + return (no MCP construction). Reuse ExternalMcpServer (src/mcp/external-client.ts); inject it so tests stub callTool. Wrap MCP calls in try/catch; sanitize errors (strip KEY=VALUE patterns as external-client.ts:69 does) and never print env/tokens. Keep now injected.
contract.evaluatorNotes: Verify the default (axis off) path makes NO MCP construction or network call — the test must assert the stub's callTool was never invoked. Verify enabling the axis + a stubbed thread payload captures one open action-Finding via captureTask (reused). Verify error sanitization: feed an error string containing a fake TOKEN=secret and assert it does not appear in the surfaced message. Confirm parseGmailThread is pure (no network/clock) and captureTask is the only write path.

principles:
# Project Principles

> These principles guide all planning, generation, and evaluation in the Bober pipeline.
> Updated: 2026-04-16

## Mission

Agent-bober is a multi-agent harness that builds applications autonomously using any LLM. It orchestrates a pipeline of specialized agents (Researcher, Planner, Curator, Generator, Evaluator) and supports Claude, GPT, Gemini, and Ollama. It ships as an npm CLI + MCP server for Claude Code, Cursor, and Windsurf.

## Users

- **Primary:** Developers using Claude Code, Cursor, or Windsurf who want autonomous feature generation with quality gates
- **Secondary:** Teams integrating agent-bober programmatically via the TypeScript API or MCP server
- Technical level: intermediate to advanced — users understand LLMs, CI/CD, and modern JS tooling

## Quality Standards

- **Type safety:** TypeScript strict mode with all strict flags (`noUnusedLocals`, `noUnusedParameters`, `noImplicitReturns`, `noFallthroughCasesInSwitch`, `isolatedModules`). Zero type errors is a hard gate.
- **Lint compliance:** ESLint flat config with `consistent-type-imports` enforced, `no-explicit-any` warned, unused vars errored (with `_` prefix escape). Zero lint errors is a hard gate.
- **Test coverage:** Vitest for unit tests. Tests are collocated with source (`*.test.ts` next to `*.ts`). Tests run against the real project when practical (e.g., scanner tests scan agent-bober itself).
- **Build integrity:** `tsc` must produce clean output. Build is a required evaluator strategy.

## Technical Principles

### Follow

- **ESM everywhere.** `"type": "module"` in package.json. All imports use `.js` extensions for NodeNext resolution. No CommonJS.
- **Provider-agnostic interfaces.** All LLM interaction goes through `providers/types.ts` — provider-specific SDKs are wrapped by adapters (`anthropic.ts`, `openai.ts`, `google.ts`). Never leak SDK types outside adapter files.
- **Zod for config validation.** All configuration schemas are Zod schemas in `config/schema.ts`. Runtime config loading uses `z.parse()`. No hand-rolled validation.
- **Contract-based architecture.** PlanSpecs, SprintContracts, and EvalResults are the communication protocol between agents. They live in `contracts/` and are JSON files on disk in `.bober/`.
- **Filesystem state.** All mutable state (specs, contracts, handoffs, eval results, history) is stored as JSON files in `.bober/`. No database, no in-memory global state.
- **Section comments.** Use unicode box-drawing section headers: `// -- Section Name ------` to organize long files.
- **Small utility modules.** Utils in `utils/` are focused single-purpose files (`fs.ts`, `git.ts`, `logger.ts`). Keep them small.
- **Conventional commits.** Format: `type: description` where type is `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `release`, `refactor`, `test`, `chore`. Sprint-generated commits use `bober(sprint-N): description`.
- **Use `type` imports.** ESLint enforces `consistent-type-imports` -- import types with `import type { ... }` syntax.
- **Prefix unused params with `_`.** The `_` prefix is the only escape hatch for unused variables/parameters.

### Avoid

- **No `any` without justification.** `no-explicit-any` is a warning; aim for zero. Use `unknown` + type narrowing instead.
- **No SDK lock-in.** Never import `@anthropic-ai/sdk` or `openai` outside of their respective adapter files in `providers/`.
- **No synchronous filesystem ops.** All fs operations use `node:fs/promises`. No `fs.readFileSync` etc.
- **No barrel re-exports for deep internals.** `src/index.ts` exports the public API only. Internal modules import directly.
- **No test mocks for filesystem.** Tests that need filesystem state create temp directories and clean up. The scanner tests run against the real codebase.

## Design Principles

N/A -- no user interface. Agent-bober is a CLI tool and programmatic library. The MCP server exposes tools, not UI.
